name: Jealousy sparks after wife photos found
summary: In an “Asking Eric” letter, a husband says he found old photos of his wife, Jane, with her childhood friend Adam inside her passport travel wallet in their safe, which triggered intense jealousy and made him wonder whether this reflects an emotional affair despite Jane and Adam reportedly seeing each other only rarely in recent years; he asks whether he should confront Jane or try to ignore it. Eric’s response, as carried by multiple outlets, argues that given the long-standing, childhood-rooted closeness described by the husband, the situation sounds more like a normal friendship than clear evidence of an emotional affair, while still leaving room for the husband to address feelings appropriately rather than assume wrongdoing. Separately, another “Dear Eric” correspondent asks whether they should contact a grieving out-of-town relative after sending a sympathy box from Amazon and not receiving a thank-you note. Eric advises letting it go, explaining that grief often delays formal etiquette and that the gift was meant as support without expectation of acknowledgment. Together, the columns emphasize interpreting evidence carefully—separating instinct from facts in relationship concerns—and extending empathy where people’s capacity to respond normally is diminished by life circumstances.
shortSummary: A reader found two photos of his wife, Jane, with her childhood friend Adam while looking for her passport and says the sight triggered intrusive doubts about an emotional (or possibly physical) affair, despite believing nothing physical happened and noting they’ve only seen each other briefly in recent years. In response, “Dear Eric” argues the closeness described—Adam as a long-term friend since childhood—makes an emotional affair seem less likely and suggests the situation may be more consistent with an innocent friendship than betrayal.

keyPoints.point: The husband describes finding *exactly six* photos inside Jane’s passport travel wallet (in their safe), including “two pictures of Jane and a friend of the opposite sex, Adam,” alongside close family and best-friend pictures.

Before they were married, Jane told him that someone once asked whether he worried about her spending so much time with Adam—*including “sleeping over at Adam’s house sometimes”*—and he recalls replying that he was not concerned because Adam was a childhood close friend.

In the “Dear Picture” reply, Eric directly states: “Based on what you wrote, I have doubts about this being an emotional affair,” adding that because Jane and Adam “have been close since childhood and remain close,” it “actually sounds quite normal” to keep such photos.

For the sympathy box case, the correspondent notes the package was not lost: “She got it because I saw the picture on her porch.” Eric then answers with: “Let it go, please,” and frames the lack of a thank-you as consistent with grief—writing that “a thank you note didn’t make it onto her to-do list today.”

name: Yamuna Vihar stolen-phone racket seized 83, arrested 2
summary: Delhi Police dismantled an inter-state racket trafficking stolen high-end mobile phones, arresting Saavej (34) and Fardeen (25) in Yamuna Vihar while they were packing a sealed carton of 83 devices for dispatch. Police said the phones—brands including Apple, Samsung, OnePlus, Vivo, Oppo and Realme—had been stolen in cases registered in Delhi and Uttar Pradesh, and their IMEI numbers were verified as stolen through the CEIR database. Investigators found that the consignment was sourced from a supplier identified as “Danish” and was being sent by courier to contacts in Bangladesh via West Bengal. The operation followed specific intelligence received around March 27, with officers conducting a planned raid of a rented premises near Momin Chowk and reaching the third floor to make the arrests. Authorities said further investigation is underway to identify other members of the network and map the full distribution chain.
shortSummary: Delhi Police dismantled an interstate mobile-phone theft and trafficking ring in Yamuna Vihar, arresting two men while they were packing 83 high-end phones to be smuggled to Bangladesh via courier routes through West Bengal. Investigators said the devices were traced to theft cases in Delhi and Uttar Pradesh and were confirmed stolen via IMEI checks, with the phones reportedly sourced from a supplier identified only as “Danish.”

keyPoints.point: Police said the planned raid was carried out at a specific address: “B-42, Gali No. 13, near Momin Chowk,” after inputs received on March 27 were verified, with officers reaching the third floor where the suspects were operating. (Times of India)
Officials identified the unit involved as the Delhi Crime Branch’s “Eastern Range-I,” which conducted the operation against the inter-state stolen mobile phone racket. (ANI via Newsable/Asianet News)
PTI reported that Saavej has a prior criminal record and has been involved in multiple cases related to “robbery, possession of stolen property and the Arms Act” in Uttar Pradesh. (PTI via Newsdrum)
Authorities said the Crime Branch team conducted the raid “carefully despite the sensitive area and the possibility of crowd gathering,” before carrying out the search and arrests. (Times of India)

name: Kyoto Animation plans July 5 Netflix premiere
summary: Kyoto Animation’s original TV anime “Sparks of Tomorrow” (20 Seiki Denki Mokuroku: Eureka Evrika) will premiere on July 5, airing on Japanese networks such as Tokyo MX and streaming worldwide exclusively on Netflix. New promotional materials unveiled at AnimeJapan 2026—including a second key visual and a new trailer/PV—preview the series’ mystery around the “20th Century Electrical Catalog” and its promise of a life beyond a smoke-choked, steam-powered world. The opening theme is “Eureka Evrika,” performed by Luna Goami, and the ending theme is by Ginger Root. The story follows a cynical boy still reeling from his brother’s death and a devoted girl carrying grief and future dreams, both drawn together to uncover the catalog’s secrets. Additional cast was announced, and a world premiere tour has been scheduled for Japan, the United Kingdom, Thailand, and North America, with more details set to be revealed in April.
shortSummary: Kyoto Animation’s new TV anime “Sparks of Tomorrow” is set to premiere July 5 in Japan (including Tokyo MX and other networks) and stream globally on Netflix, with a steam-powered mystery centered on the “20th Century Electrical Catalog.” At AnimeJapan 2026, the studio also unveiled new key visuals, additional cast, and theme songs—opening by Luna Goami and ending by Ginger Root—along with a world premiere tour scheduled across Japan, the UK, Thailand, and North America.

keyPoints.point: AnimeTV Japan reports the series will follow a weekly release schedule on Netflix (in addition to its July 5 debut), rather than releasing all episodes at once.

Oricon Group notes that at AnimeJapan 2026’s BLUE STAGE special event on March 29, the production team and cast appeared (including Yuma Uchida, Sora Amamiya, Koki Uchiyama, Daisuke Ono, Shunsuke Takeuchi, and Minako Kotobuki), and the newly released second PV runs about two minutes and 20 seconds.

Oricon Group adds specific credit details for the music: the opening theme’s lyrics are by multi singer Luna Goami, with composition by Hitomi Kotō (also responsible for the series’ soundtrack). It also describes Ginger Root as a project led by a South California–based multi-instrumentalist, producer, songwriter, and visual artist.

AniTrendz reports the anime is based on a 2018 light novel by Hiro Yūki (illustrated by Kazumi Ikeda) and adds that Netflix describes it as part of Annecy 2026’s TV Films selection; it also notes the novel received an honorable mention at the 8th Kyoto Animation Awards.

Final Weapon details key production staff: Minoru Ōta directs the project as his first directorial role, with Tatsuhiko Urahata overseeing series scripts; Kohei Okamura is chief animation director and character designer, Takaaki Suzuki is in charge of worldview setting, and Hitomi Kotō is the music composer.

name: Study finds no AI data leakage, six LLMs
summary: At Nvidia’s GTC 2026 conference, Google DeepMind and Google Research Chief Scientist Jeff Dean argued that “data wall” fears are likely overstated, saying the world still contains large amounts of untapped data—especially richer sources like robotics and autonomous-vehicle data that haven’t been widely used for training. He also pointed to synthetic data and older techniques such as data augmentation and regularization (including dropout and distillation) as practical levers to improve model performance without relying on an endless supply of new real-world datasets. Separately, a controlled study released by Search Atlas examined six major LLM platforms—OpenAI, Gemini, Perplexity, Grok, Copilot, and “Google AI Mode”—under extreme data-exposure scenarios. The study reported 0% data leakage of user-provided sensitive information across the platforms tested and found that retrieved facts disappear when search is disabled. It concludes users should focus more on the risk of AI hallucinations than on data exposure or retention of sensitive inputs.
shortSummary: Google’s Jeff Dean pushed back on claims that the AI industry is running out of training data, arguing there is plenty of untapped real-world information—especially robotics and autonomous-vehicle data—and that synthetic data and older regularization/augmentation techniques can help. A separate controlled study by Search Atlas reported 0% data leakage of user-provided sensitive information across six major LLM platforms, though it warned users are still exposed to hallucinations rather than secret data being replayed.

keyPoints.point: Jeff Dean said the industry is only training on “some video data,” arguing there is “a lot more video data — with associated audio data — that we aren’t necessarily training on yet,” as part of his pushback on the “data wall” narrative.

On synthetic data, Dean acknowledged a concern that it may just generate “variations of the same thing,” but said it still works: “It still seems to help the model sometimes, because if your model is very powerful — the one that’s generating the synthetic data — then it actually does seem to help quite a bit.”

Search Atlas reported that its controlled leakage tests used 30 purpose-built question-and-answer pairs “without any public information provided,” explicitly avoiding “search indexing, online references, or presence in the known training data.”

In the same Search Atlas methodology, the researchers posed the questions “without any prior context” and then asked “the same questions again” to check whether any user-provided sensitive information would be repeated/retained across runs.

name: Mrithyunjay Netflix streaming from April 3, 2026
summary: Sree Vishnu’s Telugu action thriller “Mrithyunjay,” written and directed by Hussain Sha Kiran, is set to make its Netflix debut on April 3, 2026, after its March 6 theatrical release. The crime-mystery centers on Jai, a newspaper marketing executive who dreams of becoming a reporter and whose curiosity about suspicious deaths—initially dismissed as accidents—pulls him into a riskier investigation. As he digs deeper, the story blends suspense with an emotional thread, including Jai’s bond with a young girl affected by her father’s mysterious death. The film stars Reba Monica John, with Veer Aaryan noted as the antagonist, and it has been positioned for a broader reach on OTT given its limited impact at the box office. Netflix will stream the movie in multiple languages, including Telugu, Tamil, Kannada, Malayalam, and Hindi.
shortSummary: Sree Vishnu’s Telugu crime thriller “Mrithyunjay,” directed by Hussain Sha Kiran and featuring Reba Monica John, will make its Netflix debut on April 3, 2026, expanding to multiple languages including Telugu, Tamil, Kannada, Malayalam, and Hindi after a March 6 theatrical run that generated positive word of mouth but limited box-office impact. The film centers on Jai, who investigates a string of suspicious deaths dismissed as accidents, blending mystery and suspense with an emotional character arc as he presses for answers.

keyPoints.point: In the film’s investigation, Jai (Sree Vishnu) seeks help from ACP Seetha Parushuram—only for her to dismiss his claims, pushing him to continue the probe on his own (as part of the story’s turning point).

The production credits include cinematography by Vidya Sagar Chinta and editing by Sreekar Prasad, with sound handled by Sync Cinemas; DI and dubbing were done at Annapurna Studios, and audio rights are with Think Music.

Prior to the Netflix announcement, the movie’s theatrical run reportedly received positive word of mouth even though it “didn’t make a strong impact at the box office,” with Netflix acquiring the streaming rights and officially confirming the digital premiere date.
Audience/critic reaction highlighted Sree Vishnu’s performance as a major strength of the film—described as “one of the film’s biggest strengths.”

name: Tems tops Spotify with 40.1m monthly listeners
summary: Nigerian singer Tems (Temilade Openiyi) has hit a major Spotify milestone, emerging as the most streamed African artist globally with roughly 40 million monthly listeners (over 40.1 million in some reports). Her breakout came through Wizkid’s “Essence,” which introduced her to a much wider international audience, and her listenership has since been boosted by high-profile collaborations, including work with Drake and Future on “Wait for U,” for which she has won a Grammy. Tems has also expanded her global footprint through contributions such as her appearance on the “Black Panther: Wakanda Forever” soundtrack. Across reports, the milestone is framed as evidence of Afrobeats’ growing mainstream reach, with Tems positioned at the forefront. Additional coverage highlights her broader acclaim, including the 2024 release of her debut album “Born in the Wild” and continued industry recognition.
shortSummary: Nigerian singer Tems has become the most streamed African artist globally on Spotify, topping Africa with just over 40 million monthly listeners. Her climb is attributed to breakthrough exposure from Wizkid’s “Essence,” major international collaborations with Drake and Future (including “Wait for U”), and her “Black Panther: Wakanda Forever” soundtrack contribution—reflecting Afrobeats’ broader mainstream momentum worldwide.

keyPoints.point: One of the reports directly quotes a statement describing Tems’ ascent as “a defining moment in her career,” adding that she has “emerg[ed] as the most streamed African artist globally on Spotify.”

Coverage notes that Tems’ breakout on Wizkid’s “Essence” is “widely credited for pushing Afrobeats further into mainstream global recognition,” linking her Spotify surge to a broader genre push rather than only individual star power.
Beyond “a Grammy,” at least one account specifies Tems has won “two Grammy Awards,” emphasizing the scale of her international recognition tied to her collaborations (including work on “Wait for U”).

Another report describes the geographical breadth of her audience growth, saying her sound resonates across “Europe, North America and other key markets,” reflecting how her listenership extends well beyond the continent.

name: Microsoft pulls Windows 11 KB5079391 update
summary: Microsoft has pulled the optional Windows 11 non-security preview update KB5079391 shortly after releasing it to production PCs on March 26, after some users reported installation problems. Reports say the update either failed to install or got stuck in an error loop, with Windows Update commonly showing error 0x80073712; Microsoft said the removal is temporary and meant to prevent further disruption while it investigates the root cause. The failure was not reported to render PCs inoperable—affected devices could continue running normally despite the update failing. Because KB5079391 was optional, it was mainly encountered by users who had enabled the “Get the latest updates as soon as they’re available” setting. The update included quality-of-life changes such as improving File Explorer’s handling of unsafe download unblocking, adding support for displays up to a 1000Hz refresh rate, and enhancing Windows Hello reliability, but Microsoft has not said when or if it will be re-released. One possibility is that Microsoft will need to revise the update (and issue a new build/KB) before making it available again.
shortSummary: Microsoft has temporarily withdrawn the optional Windows 11 non-security preview update KB5079391 after some PCs failed to install it, with affected users seeing Windows Update error 0x80073712 and the update either not installing or getting stuck. Microsoft says it is investigating the root cause and limited the update’s availability to prevent further disruption, while noting PCs were not rendered inoperable and may stop seeing the update in Windows Update during the pause.

keyPoints.point: Microsoft initially did not provide an explanation when KB5079391 was “quietly pulled within hours” of its March 26 release, which led to early speculation; two days later, the company confirmed it had discovered a problem preventing the update from installing properly on some PCs.

Reporting on the underlying failure ties the most common error (0x80073712) to missing component files: the error “means assembly files… are missing in the WinSxS folder,” and one report adds that this can leave devices “in a broken or partially updated state.”

Windows Latest reported that Microsoft told it the withdrawal was meant to prevent further impact—“To prevent additional impact while the issue is investigated, Microsoft has temporarily limited the availability of this update”—and noted the update was offered for only “about an hour or two,” with it still not downloadable as of March 28, 2026.
At least some of the affected devices were specifically identified as Windows 11 versions 25H2 and 24H2, where the update could get “stuck in an error loop.”

name: First stable Swift SDK for Android
summary: Swift 6.3, released earlier this year, includes the first official stable Swift SDK for Android, giving developers a sanctioned toolchain to build native Android apps in Swift rather than relying on unofficial workarounds (https://earthpressnews.com/swift-programming-language-officially-gains-android-support/; https://www.world-today-news.com/android-now-officially-supports-swift-development/). The SDK also ships with Swift Java and Swift Java JNI Core, which let Swift code be embedded into existing Android apps written in Kotlin or Java by handling the required Java Native Interface (JNI) bridging (https://www.cyberkendra.com/2026/03/apples-swift-programming-language-now.html; https://www.newsbytesapp.com/news/science/apple-offers-1st-official-swift-6-3-sdk-for-android/story). Cross-compilation is supported—developers can build on macOS or Linux and run on Android devices or emulators—and Swift apps still need to be packaged as standard Android .apks as part of a larger Android project (https://www.cyberkendra.com/2026/03/apples-swift-programming-language-now.html). While Android development remains centered on Kotlin and Android Studio does not yet natively support Swift, the change is positioned as opening the door for cross-platform teams to share business logic, network layers, and data models across iOS and Android without rewriting core code (https://www.cyberkendra.com/2026/03/apples-swift-programming-language-now.html; https://www.macobserver.com/news/swift-6-3-brings-official-android-support-letting-developers-build-native-apps-beyond-apple-platforms/). Swift.org and Apple engineering manager Holly Borla frame Swift 6.3 as part of the language’s broader ambition to serve multiple layers of the software stack, with the Android SDK initially aimed especially at Swift package maintainers to help prepare libraries for Android compatibility (https://www.world-today-news.com/android-now-officially-supports-swift-development/). Alongside Android support, Swift 6.3 adds other developer-focused improvements such as enhanced C interoperability via the @c attribute, new module selector capabilities, and build/testing/documentation enhancements (https://www.world-today-news.com/android-now-officially-supports-swift-development/).
shortSummary: Swift 6.3 has officially added a first stable Swift SDK for Android, enabling developers to build native Android apps in Swift and update existing Swift packages for Android without relying on experimental workarounds. The SDK also supports Swift-to-Kotlin/Java interoperability via Swift Java and Swift Java JNI Core, so developers can embed Swift code into existing Android apps while keeping Kotlin as Android’s primary language and using cross-compilation tooling for Mac/Linux-to-Android builds.

keyPoints.point: Setting up Swift 6.3’s official Android support requires three components: the Swift toolchain, the new Android SDK bundle, and Google’s Android NDK, specifically “version 27d or later,” which supplies the platform-specific headers needed to compile native code.
Cross-compilation targets both Android emulator and phone architectures: builds can produce outputs for “x86_64 (for emulators)” and “aarch64 (for most modern phones).”

Beyond the Android SDK itself, Swift 6.3 adds tooling aimed at cross-platform library workflows: it includes “a preview of the Swift Build build system integrated into the Swift Package Manager” along with improvements to Swift Testing.
Swift 6.3’s documentation generation changes include experimental DocC support for additional Markdown inputs and presentation features: “Markdown, static HTML content, and code block annotations.”

Swift.org/Apple positioning emphasizes library readiness for Android: the initial Android SDK rollout is “primarily intended for Swift package maintainers,” and the article notes that “currently, only approximately 25% of packages available on the Swift…” are prepared/compatible enough for Android (per the same source).

name: California juries find Meta, YouTube liable, $6m
summary: A California jury found Meta and YouTube negligent for designing platforms in ways that can be addictive to minors, focusing on features like infinite scroll and autoplay rather than specific content, and ordered about $6 million in damages to the plaintiff. Reporting on the trial says jurors credited evidence that the companies studied potential addictive risks for young users yet continued engagement-driving design choices, including discussions about beauty-filter restrictions for teens. Legal analysts say the verdicts—along with a separate New Mexico case where a jury found Meta liable under the state’s consumer-protection law—could give new momentum to kids’ online safety litigation as Congress stalls on regulation. The Hill notes the awards do not automatically force platform redesigns but may encourage broader changes and additional suits. In contrast, a psychiatrist writing for Psychology Today argues the ruling oversimplifies addiction by downplaying personal responsibility and the role of multiple risk factors (such as mental health conditions, genetics, and environment), and notes that “social media addiction” is not currently a formal diagnosis in the DSM-5 due to insufficient evidence of clinically significant impairment. Overall, the cases are being framed as a possible turning point in how courts assess whether tech companies can be held responsible for the psychological harms linked to “dark patterns.”
shortSummary: A California jury found Meta and YouTube/Google negligent for designing addictive social-media features (including infinite scroll/autoplay) after a teen user said the habit worsened her mental health, awarding about $6 million, while a separate New Mexico verdict against Meta ordered $375 million tied to children’s online safety and unfair-deceptive practices. A psychiatrist writing in Psychology Today argues the ruling is overly simplistic because “social media addiction” is not a formal DSM-5 diagnosis and risk is likely driven by multiple factors beyond platform design alone.

keyPoints.point: Jurors reportedly said Meta and YouTube acted with “malice” and emphasized they “failed to warn” the plaintiff about the risks of the platforms’ design; the article also says damages were split into $3 million compensatory and $3 million punitive, with Meta paying 70% and YouTube 30%.

Reporting on the California trial states it took jurors 40 hours over nine days to reach a verdict, and that executives—including Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg—testified to defend the companies’ design decisions.

The Hill reports the California case involved a consolidated mass of suits (by “individuals, school districts and states”) and quotes attorney Allison Fitzpatrick calling the verdict a “wake-up call” that the “status quo is no longer sufficient,” urging steps to protect children and teens online “including on social media platforms.”

The Hill adds specific details from the parallel New Mexico case: a jury found Meta liable for compromising children’s safety online and violating the state’s Unfair Practices Act, awarding $375 million in damages.
One report frames the verdict as part of a larger wave, saying there are “more than 1,500 similar cases” against social media companies awaiting trial in the United States.

name: iShares SUB short interest rises 79% March
summary: Across two intermediate-term corporate bond ETFs, the Vanguard and iShares options look broadly similar on cost, yield, and long-term risk, with differences mainly in how concentrated the portfolios are and their scale—Vanguard holds more bonds and has far more assets, while iShares leans more toward financials. For investors comparing corporate vs. municipal income, the Vanguard Short-Term Corporate Bond ETF (VCSH) emphasizes high-grade, taxable corporate debt with a lower fee and higher yield than the VanEck Short Muni ETF (SMB), while SMB targets federally tax-exempt short-duration municipal bonds and spreads exposure across hundreds of holdings. A similar tradeoff appears when weighing corporate credit risk against government-backed safety: VCSH and Schwab’s Short-Term U.S. Treasury ETF (SCHO) both charge about 0.03%, but VCSH’s yield is higher because it holds investment-grade corporates, whereas SCHO is concentrated in Treasuries (reducing credit risk). When choosing between Treasuries and a diversified municipal sleeve, VGIT (Treasuries) carries a slight fee advantage and a somewhat higher yield than MUB, but MUB has shown less drawdown over the prior five years and provides broader muni diversification with tax benefits. Overall, the comparisons highlight that deciding between these ETF types often comes down to whether investors value taxable yield and corporate credit exposure or prioritize municipal tax advantages and diversification, versus seeking the steadier profile of Treasury-heavy portfolios.
shortSummary: Recent ETF comparisons in short- and intermediate-term bond funds find that low-fee options can be broadly similar on cost and short-term results, but diverge meaningfully in credit risk, diversification, and tax treatment: Vanguard’s and iShares’ intermediate corporate ETFs largely match on yield/risk and fees, while VCSH (taxable short-term corporates) is positioned for higher income than tax-exempt municipal peers like SMB, and it carries more corporate credit exposure than Treasury-focused SCHO; similarly, VGIT (Treasuries) and MUB (diversified municipals) differ in yield and drawdown history with municipals benefiting from broader diversification and potential tax advantages. Separately, iShares’ SUB (short-term national munis) saw a sharp jump in short interest in mid-March, alongside generally steady trading and small amounts of shares short-sold.

keyPoints.point: In the intermediate-term corporate-bond ETF comparison, Vanguard’s fund holds 2,289 bonds with more than half in industrial-sector bonds, while the iShares fund holds 3,001 dollar-denominated corporate bonds with about a quarter of assets in major-bank financials. Both ETFs avoid leverage and any ESG screens, but iShares charges 0.04% vs Vanguard’s 0.03%, and Vanguard manages ~$68B vs iShares’ ~$17B in assets.

For VCSH vs SMB, SMB’s expense ratio is higher (0.07% vs VCSH’s 0.03%) and it invests in more than 300 short-term municipal bonds—citing top holdings such as California Community Choice Financing A, New York City Transitional Finance Authority, and State of California—while VCSH is much more concentrated, holding just 12 positions (and listing holdings such as “U.S. Dollar” and a United States Treasury Note/Bond).

For VCSH vs SCHO, the Schwab Treasury ETF is described as allocating 96% of assets to cash and Treasuries and holding 98 securities (with top positions in Treasury Notes), positioning it as government-backed with minimal credit risk; by contrast, VCSH is described as focusing exclusively on investment-grade corporate bonds and cash and (again) holding only 12 positions, which drives higher credit exposure.

In the VGIT vs MUB comparison, the cited specifics (as of March 24, 2026) are: VGIT’s expense ratio is 0.03% vs MUB’s 0.05%, VGIT’s dividend yield is about 3.8% vs MUB’s 3.2%, and MUB’s five-year maximum drawdown is materially smaller (-11.89% vs VGIT’s -15.01%). The articles also note MUB holds 6,300+ investment-grade muni bonds versus VGIT’s concentration in 76 U.S. Treasury notes and bonds.

Separately, one market-sentiment datapoint: iShares Short-Term National Muni Bond ETF (SUB) saw short interest rise sharply in March, reaching 263,732 shares as of March 13—up 79.4% from February 26 (147,032 shares). The short-interest ratio was reported at 0.4 days, and about 0.3% of the fund’s shares were reported as short sold.
